item.title: Spain heat wave kills over 1,000 in second-hottest June ever
item.description: [Climate & Environment] Keyword: climate change — At least 1,028 people died during Spain's recent heat wave as the country endured its hottest January-to-June period on record. Scientists said the extreme heat across Europe was driven by climate change.

item.title: In Germany, political fallout from heat wave begins
item.description: [Climate & Environment] Keyword: climate change — Following a major heat wave, critics say the government needs to do more to adapt to climate change. What can policymakers do to ensure the country is better prepared for extreme temperatures in the future?

item.title: World's oceans break June heat record: EU monitor
item.description: [Climate & Environment] Keyword: climate change — The world's oceans just experienced their hottest June on record and could set fresh highs in the months ahead as El Niño and climate change drive temperatures even higher, scientists said Wednesday.

item.title: Sudan's RSF committed crimes against humanity in el-Fasher, Amnesty says
item.description: [AI & Automation] Keyword: LLM — The Amnesty report adds to evidence of atrocities in el-Fasher, which the UN says bore the hallmarks of genocide.

item.title: Ocean surface temperatures hit a record high for June
item.description: [Climate & Environment] Keyword: climate change — European scientists warn of consequences for weather patterns, the global climate and marine lifeTemperatures on the ocean surface have hit a record high, raising fears of another burst of extreme heat this summer.On 21 June, temperatures outside the polar regions exceeded the extraordinary highs observed at the same time in 2023 and 2024, the Copernicus Climate Change Service said on Wednesday. Continue reading...

item.title: Venezuela: Death toll nears 2,000 as rescue hopes fade
item.description: [Cannabis, Hemp & Drug Policy] Keyword: THCA — After the quakes, almost 2,000 people are confirmed dead, and about 43,000 remain missing. Aid agencies warn of worsening hunger and disease amid an overwhelmed healthcare system.

item.title: Mice actively seek better views to make visual decisions, virtual reality experiments show
item.description: [Science & Research] Keyword: neuroscience — Animals don't experience the world passively. A hawk tilts its head to track prey. A person leans forward to read a sign. Scientists call this "active sensing": moving the body to gather better information. A specific version of active sensing is infotaxis, which describes how animals move strategically to maximize the information they gain from their surroundings. Whether mice use this strategy has remained an open question, despite their central role in neuroscience research.

item.title: Warming can shift freshwater crustaceans to a 'greener' diet
item.description: [Climate & Environment] Keyword: climate change — Climate change is not only warming our lakes and rivers, it is also changing what invasive species eat. A new experimental study published in Limnology and Oceanography Letters shows that temperature-driven diet shifts in an invasive crustacean could alter its ecological role in freshwater food webs.

item.title: Black Seminole and Lipan Apache Ndé Groups Sign Historic Treaty During Juneteenth Celebration
item.description: [Indigenous News & Tribal Sovereignty] Keyword: Indigenous — The treaty highlights how colonial dispossession of both Indigenous and Black communities damaged generational relations among peoples. Does it also point the way toward healing our broken relationship with water?

item.title: Germany's heat wave has a political fallout
item.description: [Climate & Environment] Keyword: climate change — In the wake of the heat wave, critics say the government needs to do more to adapt to climate change.

item.title: Industrial-era pollution and warming reshape Tibetan lake after 1,000 years of climate swings
item.description: [Climate & Environment] Keyword: climate change — The Tibetan Plateau, together with the Hindu Kush–Karakorum–Himalaya region, has more snow and ice than any other region on Earth apart from the polar regions. As a result, this high-altitude region is particularly sensitive to climate change, making it especially important in analyzing its impacts. In recent years, researchers from the DFG Research Training Group TransTiP have been investigating changes in the region's geo-ecosystems.

item.title: Healthcare situation in DR Congo improves amidst Ebola crisis
item.description: [Cannabis, Hemp & Drug Policy] Keyword: THCA — The Ebola virus continues to spread across the DR Congo but local healthcare workers say the situation has improved.

item.title: Arabian Sea sediments reveal summer and winter monsoons shifted differently after last ice age
item.description: [Climate & Environment] Keyword: climate change — High-resolution sediment analyses from the Arabian Sea reveal, for the first time, that summer and winter monsoons respond differently to global climate change. The study enhances understanding of past precipitation patterns and could help refine climate models for regions influenced by monsoons.

item.title: Study: Europe Heat Wave “Virtually Impossible” Without Human-Made Climate Change
item.description: [Climate & Environment] Keyword: climate change — The current heat wave would have been significantly cooler even just 50 years ago, the study points out.

item.title: Tiny ancient fish fossil with preserved brain offers clues to early fish evolution
item.description: [Open Source Software] Keyword: FOSS — Over 300 million years ago, a minnow-sized fish died and fell to the bottom of a prehistoric swamp near the village of Trawden, Lancashire, in northwest England. The remains of this tiny fish—known as Trawdenia planti—became fossilized, embedding proof of its existence in a layer of soapstone sandwiched between coal seams in the Burnley coalfields. By some combination of marine chemistry, mineral composition of the seafloor, timing and luck, not only was the bony skeleton of this fish preserved,

item.title: The New Five Forces, Part 4: Environment and Its Regulation
item.description: [AI & Automation] Keyword: LLM — [This is the fourth part of a five-part series adapted from Dr. Noa Gafni’s report, The New Five Forces: A Blueprint for Business in an Uncertain World. To read more, see Parts 1, 2 and 3 here.] In the third installment of this series on the New Five Forces, we examined how societal expectations, trust… Continue reading The New Five Forces, Part 4: Environment and Its Regulation The post The New Five Forces, Part 4: Environment and Its Regulation appeared first on Fair Observer.

item.title: Meet Aber Kawas, DSA-Backed Palestinian American Who Won New York State Senate Primary
item.description: [Cannabis, Hemp & Drug Policy] Keyword: THCA — The Democratic Socialists of America’s slate dominated the New York primaries last week, with Aber Kawas winning the Democratic nomination for a New York state Senate seat in the New York City borough of Queens with a 20-point lead against progressive State Assemblymember Steven Raga. Born and raised in New York to Palestinian parents, Kawas campaigned on affordable housing, universal healthcare, immigration reform, public transit, climate action and opposition to U.S. support for Israel&#

item.title: Climate change, ‘super-strong’ El Niño may push Hong Kong to record-high temperatures, Observatory warns
item.description: [Climate & Environment] Keyword: climate change — The Hong Kong Observatory (HKO) has warned that the city may experience record-breaking high temperatures this year and next year due to climate change and forecasts of a “super-strong” El Niño event. “According to the latest forecasts from multiple climate models, sea temperatures in the central and eastern equatorial Pacific will continue to rise, developing […]

item.title: Did US drug agents allow lethal fentanyl to hit New Mexico’s streets?
item.description: [Opioid Treatment & Methadone] Keyword: fentanyl — Explosive AP story based on whistleblower testimony suggests agents ‘sat back and watched’ in hopes of securing larger drug-trafficking bustDid the Drug Enforcement Agency break the law and gamble with public safety when it permitted large quantities of fentanyl pills to be trafficked in New Mexico in the hopes of getting a larger drug-trafficking bust?That is the question at the heart of an explosive story published in the Associated Press, based on information provided by a former DEA agent tu

item.title: What Happened in Rural News This Month?
item.description: [Immigration & Border] Keyword: border wall — How closely have you been following the latest stories from rural communities across the country? From migrating birds and Superman to data centers and border walls, this month’s news highlights the challenges, celebrations, and resistance shaping rural life. Take this short quiz to see what you know. The post What Happened in Rural News This Month? appeared first on The Daily Yonder.

item.title: “Save Our Bacon” Act Would Bar States From Regulating Factory Farm Cruelty
item.description: [USDA & Farm Grants] Keyword: farm bill — This story was originally published by Grist and is reproduced here as part of the Climate Desk collaboration. It’s been nearly eight years since Congress reauthorized the farm bill, the massive legislative package that funds programs run by the US Department of Agriculture. What used to be passed roughly every five years, the farm bill touches nearly every aspect of […]

item.title: Commentary: What Rural Health Innovation Needs to Succeed
item.description: [Cannabis, Hemp & Drug Policy] Keyword: THCA — In southeastern Kentucky, where provider shortages, persistent poverty, and geographic isolation continue to shape daily life, one rural health center is quietly building a healthcare workforce from within its own community. This month, Mountain Comprehensive Health Corporation (MCHC) will graduate another class of healthcare workers through its Reach Achieve Inspire, Support, Employ (RAISE) workforce development program — helping […] The post Commentary: What Rural Health I

item.title: Scientists say creatine may help fight depression
item.description: [Science & Research] Keyword: clinical trial — Creatine is best known as a muscle-building supplement, but scientists are now investigating whether it could also help treat depression by boosting the brain's energy supply. A new review examined five randomized clinical trials involving 238 participants and found mixed results. Two studies, both involving women with major depressive disorder, reported that adding creatine to standard treatment improved symptoms, while three others found no meaningful benefit.

item.title: LGBT Q&A: What Data Are Companies in the UK Collecting When Verifying My Age?
item.description: [Digital Rights & Privacy] Keyword: digital rights — This Pride, we’re answering all your digital rights questions in season two of our initiative, LGBT Q&A. You Asked: I live in the UK, and we have age verification now on a bunch of websites (including Reddit) and now on iPhones. Can you explain what sort of data companies are actually collecting when they check for age and whether there are any real threats to my safety? EFF’s Answer: Age verification is a process where a website or service checks your age to determine whether a user is ov

item.description: [AI & Automation] Keyword: large language model — Generative artificial intelligence (AI), especially large language models deployed as chatbots and digital assistants, are now part of everyday digital life.

item.title: The 20km ripple effect: How mines can trigger distant deforestation in Africa
item.description: [Climate & Environment] Keyword: deforestation — The global push for a transition to green energy has sparked demand for critical minerals such as lithium, vanadium, copper and cobalt. These are needed for electric vehicles, wind turbines and solar panels. Sub-Saharan Africa hosts about 30% of the world's mineral reserves, including huge quantities of critical minerals: 92% of all platinum, 36% of all chromium, 54% of all manganese and 56% of all cobalt.

item.title: Unexpected pathway turns water and CO₂ into climate‑neutral methane on nickel–zirconia
item.description: [Open Source Software] Keyword: FOSS — Natural gas still plays an important role in many industrial sectors, but it is a climate-damaging fossil fuel. TU Wien and the University of Innsbruck have now discovered an unexpected reaction pathway that makes it possible to synthesize natural gas, or methane (CH4), using CO2 that was previously captured from exhaust gas streams or directly from the air. In this way, methane can become climate-neutral overall.

item.title: How Texas law firms are redefining personal injury representation standards
item.description: [Texas & San Antonio Local] Keyword: San Antonio — SPONSORED: The standard for personal injury representation continues to evolve as Texas firms strengthen how they handle client cases. It's still important to closely examine your options.How Texas law firms are redefining personal injury representation standards was first posted on June 29, 2026 at 6:00 pm.©2021 "San Antonio Report". Use of this feed is for personal non-commercial use only. If you are not reading this article in your feed reader, then the site is guilty of copyri

item.title: Testing the orbital mechanics of giant mirrors
item.description: [AI & Automation] Keyword: LLM — Giant mirrors in space have been a staple of science fiction for decades. But so far, there's been very little work looking at the actual physics behind the concept—possibly because we're still so far from making them ourselves. Still, they could potentially serve as a passive technosignature if we manage to find one. In order to do that, though, we have to understand what we're looking for. That is the purpose of a new paper, available as a preprint on arXiv, by Shauna Sallmen of the University

item.title: Deep inside crocodile skulls, 100 million years of brain evolution barely registers
item.description: [Open Source Software] Keyword: FOSS — Although modern crocodiles, alligators, caimans and gharials are restricted to the tropics, their fossil record tells a very different story. Ancient crocodylians once inhabited much of the globe and exhibited a remarkable diversity of skull shapes and ecological adaptations.

item.title: Q&A: What happens when warming streams push young salmon beyond their limits
item.description: [Climate & Environment] Keyword: climate change — As climate change warms rivers across British Columbia, young salmon are facing increasing heat stress at vulnerable stages of their lives. Two studies from UBC's Pacific Salmon Ecology and Conservation Lab have found that younger fish cope with heat differently than older fish and that current methods may be underestimating the risks salmon face in warming waterways.

item.title: The military used the technology to scan for land mines. San Antonio is using it to detect lead pipes.

San Antonio is using it to detect lead pipes. was first posted on June 29, 2026 at 2:21 pm.©2021 "San Antonio Report". Use of this feed is for personal non-commercial use only. item.title: First ever dinosaur found in Antarctica described for science
item.description: [Open Source Software] Keyword: FOSS — The first dinosaur fossil found on the Antarctic continent has been described scientifically. The fossil, a vertebra, was found on a British Antarctic Survey (BAS) expedition in 1985 but has only recently been recognized as that of a dinosaur. The paper, "A titanosaurian sauropod dinosaur from the Late Cretaceous of Antarctica," is published in the journal Acta Palaeontologica Polonica.

item.title: Luigi Mangione’s federal trial now scheduled for January 2027 to not clash with state case
item.description: [Cannabis, Hemp & Drug Policy] Keyword: THCA — Mangione, accused of killing health executive Brian Thompson, has pleaded not guilty in state and federal casesLuigi Mangione ’s Manhattan federal court trial related to to the murder of UnitedHealthcare executive Brian Thompson is scheduled to start in January 2027.Mangione stands accused of the 4 December 2024 killing of Thompson on a New York City street. He faces murder and weapons charges in his state-level case, and stalking counts in the federal proceedings. Continue reading...

item.title: Are We Building the Infrastructure of Unfreedom?
item.description: [Climate & Environment] Keyword: climate change — How many big issues should our civilization be concerned about today? Whatever the number, most will agree that there are now too many. Concern for the environment and climate change has been around for decades. Europe over the past two months has endured record-breaking heat waves. The liberating joy associated with late spring sunshine accompanied… Continue reading Are We Building the Infrastructure of Unfreedom? The post Are We Building the Infrastructure of Unfreedom? appeared first on

item.title: EV myths muddy the road to electrification
item.description: [Open Source Software] Keyword: FOSS — Claims that electric vehicle batteries are tainted by exploitative mineral supply chains are discrediting non-fossil cars as electrified road transport booms.

item.title: Mageia 10 released
item.description: [Open Source Software] Keyword: Linux kernel — Mageia 10 has been released with the 6.18 Linux kernel, DNF 5.4.0, RPM 4.20.1, and an increase in hardware requirements for x86 32-bit systems; users now need a CPU with SSE2 features. See the release notes for a full list of updates, and the errata page for known problems.

item.title: Europe heatwave shows need to reject climate denial ‘lies’, says EU green chief
item.description: [Open Source Software] Keyword: FOSS — Teresa Ribera blames ‘ideologically driven’ falsehoods, driven by those with vested interests in fossil fuels, for attacks on green policyThe heatwave wreaking chaos across Europe is a “dramatic warning” to reject climate naysayers, a European Commission vice-president says.Teresa Ribera, executive vice-president for a clean, just and competitive transition, lambasted those who listened to the “vested interests” of the fossil fuel industry rather than scientists and their own citizens. Continue

item.title: Lebanon’s displaced begin to return home, but some have nowhere to go
item.description: [Housing & Gentrification] Keyword: displacement — Thousands in Lebanon attempt to return home, yet rampant destruction leaves many trapped in displacement centres.

item.title: US military races to vaccinate new recruits before flu shots expire
item.description: [Texas & San Antonio Local] Keyword: San Antonio — Move comes amid growing outbreak in Texas and after two-month halt on mandatory shotsThe US military is racing to vaccinate new recruits after a two-month halt on mandatory flu shots – but it’s a temporary reprieve, as the shots will soon expire and new doses will not be available for months.Officials will need to lean on other prevention measures to contain the growing flu outbreak at Lackland air force base in San Antonio, Texas, experts say. Continue reading...

item.title: The New Five Forces, Part 3: Society Expects Accountability
item.description: [AI & Automation] Keyword: LLM — [This is the third part of a five-part series adapted from Dr. Noa Gafni’s report, The New Five Forces: A Blueprint for Business in an Uncertain World. To read more, see Parts 1 and 2 here.] In the second installment of this series on the New Five Forces, we examined how geopolitics has become a… Continue reading The New Five Forces, Part 3: Society Expects Accountability The post The New Five Forces, Part 3: Society Expects Accountability appeared first on Fair Observer.

item.title: Camera traps reveal the true culprit behind crop damage in Honduras
item.description: [Ecology & Conservation] Keyword: wildlife conservation — A new study from the Honduran Mosquitia shows how simple, noninvasive technology can help solve one of the most common challenges in wildlife conservation: identifying the species actually responsible for crop damage. The full detailed results have been published in Neotropical Biology and Conservation.

item.title: Universities Join Hands to Enhance Agroforestry Research for Mitigating Climate Change
item.description: [Climate & Environment] Keyword: climate change — A team of universities, led by Addis Ababa University, has joined forces to implement a four-year Intra-Africa academic mobility project aimed at strengthening agroforestry research and education for climate change mitigation. The project, dubbed Strengthening Agroforestry Research and Education for Climate Change Mitigation in Africa (SERA), brings together JKUAT (Kenya) and Addis Ababa University (Ethiopia) […]

item.title: Offshore Oil and Gas Rush Threatens Whale Corridors and Coral Reefs
item.description: [Open Source Software] Keyword: FOSS — From coral reefs in Kenya to Caribbean seagrass meadows and whale migration corridors in the Arctic, a surge in offshore oil, gas and liquefied natural gas development is spreading into some of the world’s most ecologically important marine habitats, according to a new analysis. In many cases, researchers from Earth Insight—a nonprofit that maps fossil […]

item.title: Five Americans die every hour from toxic vehicle emissions, study finds
item.description: [Open Source Software] Keyword: FOSS — Research suggests 41,800 premature US deaths in 2024 were attributable to road pollutionRoughly five Americans die every hour due to exposure to toxic road vehicle pollution, a new study has found.It’s the latest warning showing fossil-fueled transit is a major driver of mortality. Continue reading...

item.title: Supreme court nears the end of its term with cases about Donald Trump’s power to be decided – US politics live
item.description: [Open Source Software] Keyword: FOSS — Court expected to hand down decisions on several outstanding cases, wrapping up term that has focussed on Trump’s expansive claims of presidential powerSign up for the Breaking News US emailThe Trump administration is attempting to shrink public comment periods for fossil fuel leasing on federal land while shifting the financial risks of cleanup to taxpayers and allowing for more planet-warming emissions. It’s part of a broader effort to dismantle public input processes and save polluting compan

item.title: Germany: At least 5 killed in shooting in Stade
item.description: [Immigration & Border] Keyword: ICE operation — A large police operation is underway in the northern German city after several shots were fired. Several people are reported dead and authorities say it is a "dynamic situation."

item.title: Q&A: NEISD’s newest leader talks challenges, AI and changing public education landscape
item.description: [Texas & San Antonio Local] Keyword: San Antonio — The Report sat down with NEISD's new superintendent to talk about challenges facing public education amid a wave of changes. Q&A: NEISD’s newest leader talks challenges, AI and changing public education landscape was first posted on June 29, 2026 at 5:00 am.©2021 "San Antonio Report".
